**ML SYSTEM DESIGN**

**The 9-Step Formula for Production-Grade ML Systems**

*A Complete Study Guide with Examples, Tips & Interview Strategy*

# **What Is ML System Design?**

Machine learning system design is the process of designing a complete production-level ML-powered product—not just training a model with good accuracy, but architecting every component needed to build, serve, monitor, and improve it in the real world.

| The Big Distinction: Research ML: Train a model. Get good metrics. Publish. Production ML: Train a model. Serve it to millions. Monitor it. Retrain it. Handle failures. Explain it. Scale it. ML System Design interviews test the SECOND kind. That is what this guide is about. |
| :---- |

Deploying a deep learning model in production requires several distinct components working together:

* Data pipeline — collection, labeling, storage, and preprocessing

* Feature engineering — converting raw data into model-ready numbers

* Model development — selecting, training, and evaluating the right model

* Prediction service — serving predictions at low latency and high throughput

* Online testing — A/B testing, canary releases, shadow deployment

* Monitoring — tracking data drift, model degradation, system health

* Continuous retraining — keeping the model fresh as the world changes

# **How to Approach an ML System Design Interview**

ML system design interviews are open-ended — there is no single correct answer. The interviewer is evaluating your ability to:

1. Translate a vague business problem into a concrete ML problem

2. Design a system that works at scale, not just in a notebook

3. Make and justify trade-off decisions under constraints

4. Communicate clearly — think out loud, ask clarifying questions

5. Know when NOT to use ML (sometimes rule-based is better)

| Interview strategy tip: Always start by clarifying requirements before jumping into solutions. Interviewers reward engineers who ask the right questions. 'What is the scale? What is the latency requirement? What does success look like?' These questions show maturity. |
| :---- |

# **The 9-Step ML System Design Formula**

This is your playbook for every ML system design problem — in interviews and at work. Follow these steps in order, but be flexible: skip steps if the interviewer is not interested, or go deep on one or two if they ask for it.

| Step | Name | What You Are Deciding |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| 1 | Problem Formulation | What is the ML task? What are the inputs and outputs? |
| 2 | Metrics (Offline \+ Online) | How do we know if the system is working? |
| 3 | Architectural Components | What are the main building blocks of the system? |
| 4 | Data Collection & Preparation | Where does the data come from? How is it labeled? |
| 5 | Feature Engineering | How do we convert raw data into numbers for the model? |
| 6 | Model Development & Offline Evaluation | Which model? How do we train and evaluate it? |
| 7 | Prediction Service | How do we serve predictions fast and reliably? |
| 8 | Online Testing & Deployment | How do we safely roll out the model to users? |
| 9 | Scaling, Monitoring & Updates | How do we keep the system healthy and improving? |

| 1 | Problem Formulation *Translate a business problem into a precise ML task* |
| :---: | :---- |

This is the most important step. Get this wrong, and everything else is built on a broken foundation.

### **1A — Clarifying Questions to Ask**

A **senior** engineer always asks before designing. These questions show you understand what matters in production:

* What is the business goal? (engagement, revenue, safety, efficiency?)

* Who are the users? How many? What latency do they expect?

* What data is available? Volume? Quality? Freshness?

* Are there constraints? (budget, legal, privacy, latency?)

* What does failure look like? (What is the worst outcome?)

* Is real-time prediction required, or can we batch process?

### **1B — Translate to an ML Problem**

After clarifying, you define the ML task precisely:

| Component | What to Define | Example |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| ML Objective | What the model should optimize for | Maximize probability that a user clicks a recommended video |
| ML Input | What the model receives | User profile \+ video metadata \+ context |
| ML Output | What the model predicts | Probability of click (0 to 1 float) |
| ML Category | The type of ML task | Binary classification |

### **1C — ML Categories Reference**

| Category | When to Use | Output Type | Example Problems |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Binary Classification | Yes/No decision | Single probability | Spam detection, fraud detection, harmful content |
| Multi-class Classification | One of N categories | Probability vector (N classes) | Image recognition, language detection |
| Multi-label Classification | Multiple categories at once | N independent probabilities | Content tagging, disease diagnosis |
| Multi-task Learning | Multiple related tasks, shared features | N outputs from shared model | Harmful content (violence \+ nudity \+ hate) |
| Regression | Predict a continuous number | Float value | ETA prediction, house price, ad bid |
| Ranking / LTR | Order items by relevance | Ranked list | Search results, news feed, recommendations |
| Embedding / Retrieval | Find similar items efficiently | Dense vector | Semantic search, candidate generation |
| Sequence-to-Sequence | Transform one sequence to another | Output sequence | Machine translation, summarization |

| Do we even need ML? Ask this question out loud in the interview. If the problem can be solved with simple rules (e.g., 'block all posts containing a known list of slurs'), do that first. ML is expensive. Only use it when the problem is too complex for rules or when you have labeled data and scale justifies the investment. |
| :---- |

| 2 | Metrics (Offline and Online) *Define how you know the system is succeeding* |
| :---: | :---- |

Metrics are the language of alignment between ML engineers and product managers. Define them clearly before building anything.

### **2A — Offline Metrics (Measured During Development)**

These are computed on a held-out test set before the model goes to production.

**Classification Metrics**

| Metric | Formula | When to Use |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Accuracy | Correct predictions/total | Only when classes are balanced (rare in practice) |
| Precision | TP / (TP \+ FP) | When false positives are costly (spam filter: don't delete good emails) |
| Recall | TP / (TP \+ FN) | When false negatives are costly (cancer detection: don't miss cases) |
| F1 Score | 2 × Precision × Recall / (P \+ R) | When you need a single number balancing precision and recall |
| ROC-AUC | Area under TPR vs FPR curve | Imbalanced data — overall discrimination ability |
| PR-AUC | Area under Precision vs Recall curve | Highly imbalanced data (rare positives) — more informative than ROC |
| Log-Loss | −(y log p \+ (1−y) log(1−p)) | When confidence calibration matters, not just ranking |

**Ranking & Retrieval Metrics**

| Metric | What It Measures | Best For |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Precision@K | Relevant items in top K results | When top results matter most (search) |
| Recall@K | Relevant items found in top K | When coverage matters (candidate generation) |
| mAP | Mean Average Precision across queries | Binary relevance, e.g. document retrieval |
| MRR | Mean Reciprocal Rank of first hit | When only the first correct result matters |
| nDCG | Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain | Graded relevance — better results higher up get more credit |

**Other Offline Metrics**

* Regression: MSE (mean squared error), MAE (mean absolute error), RMSE

* NLP tasks: BLEU (translation quality), ROUGE (summarization), GLUE (general NLP benchmark)

* Computational cost: inference latency (p50, p95, p99), memory footprint, FLOPs

### **2B — Online Metrics (Measured in Production)**

These are business-level signals measured on real users. They tell you if the model is actually helping.

| Metric | What It Captures | Common Use Cases |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Click-Through Rate (CTR) | Fraction of shown items that get clicked | Recommendations, ads, search |
| Engagement Rate | Likes, comments, shares per impression | Social media feed, content ranking |
| Conversion Rate | Actions taken (purchase, signup) per session | E-commerce, ads, onboarding |
| Session Length / Watch Time | How long users engage per session | Video recommendations, music |
| Task Success Rate | Fraction of sessions where user completed their goal | Search, Q\&A, navigation |
| Revenue Lift | Revenue change vs control group | Ads, pricing, upsell |
| Counter Metrics (negative) | Hide rate, skip rate, report rate | Content quality, safety |

| Key Trade-Off: Optimizing only for CTR can lead to clickbait. Optimizing only for watch time can surface addictive content. Always define counter metrics (negative feedback signals) alongside positive ones. A healthy system maximizes positive engagement while keeping negative signals low. |
| :---- |

| 3 | Architectural Components (MVP Logic) *Design the high-level system and its main building blocks* |
| :---: | :---- |

This is the whiteboard moment. Sketch the full system — every component and how they connect. Think in two layers:

* Non-ML components: API servers, databases, message queues, CDNs, user-facing interfaces

* ML components: candidate generators, rankers, classifiers, embedding stores, feature pipelines

### **3A — Common ML System Architectures**

| System Type | Typical Architecture Pattern | Examples |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Recommendation | Candidate Generation → Ranking → Re-Ranking → Filter | YouTube, Netflix, Spotify |
| Search | Query Processing → Retrieval (ANN) → Ranking → Result Rendering | Google, LinkedIn Search |
| Content Moderation | Feature Extraction → Multi-Task Classifier → Enforcement Tiers | Facebook, YouTube Safety |
| Ads Serving | Targeting → Candidate Retrieval → Bid Prediction → Auction → Ranking | Google Ads, Meta Ads |
| Newsfeed Ranking | Candidate Pool → Engagement Predictor → Diversity Filter → Feed | Facebook, Twitter/X |

### **3B — MVP Architecture Sketch Template**

![][image1]

| Interview Tip: Always draw this diagram on the whiteboard first. Say 'Here is my MVP architecture. Let me walk through each component.' Then go deeper on whichever component the interviewer shows most interest in. |
| :---- |

| 4 | Data Collection and Preparation *Where data comes from and how it gets labeled* |
| :---: | :---- |

### **4A — Data Needs Assessment**

Before collecting data, define what you need:

* Target variable: What does the model predict? Is it available in the data?

* Key actors: Who or what are the main entities? (users, items, documents, queries, ads)

* Data types: Structured (tabular) or unstructured (text, image, video, audio)?

* Volume: How much data is needed? How much is available?

* Freshness: How quickly does the data become stale?

### **4B — Labeling Methods**

| Method | How It Works | Quality | Cost | Best For |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Natural Labels | Use implicit user actions (clicks, likes, purchases, watch time) | Noisy — clicking ≠ loving | Free — already logged | Large-scale training data |
| Explicit User Feedback | Ratings, thumbs up/down, survey responses | Higher quality | Low cost | When users voluntarily give feedback |
| Human Annotation | Paid contractors manually label data | Gold standard | Very expensive \+ slow | Evaluation datasets, safety data |
| Weak Supervision | Heuristics, regex, keyword lists, other models as labels | Noisy but consistent | Cheap \+ fast | When hand-labeling at scale is impossible |
| Semi-supervised | Label small set → propagate to unlabeled data via perturbation/similarity | Moderate | Medium | When labeled data is scarce |
| Transfer Learning | Pre-train on large dataset, fine-tune on task | Very High | Medium | NLP and vision tasks with limited data |

### **4C — Handling Missing Negative Labels**

| The Missing Negative Problem: In recommendation systems, a user not clicking a video does NOT mean they disliked it — they may never have seen it. This is a critical problem called 'missing not at random' (MNAR). Naive negative sampling (treating all unseen items as negative) introduces heavy bias. Solution: random negative sampling, popularity-weighted negative sampling, or explicit user-provided negative signals (e.g., 'not interested' button). |
| :---- |

### **4D — Data Splits**

* Standard split: 70% train / 15% validation / 15% test

* Time-correlated data: ALWAYS split by time — never shuffle. Future data leaking into training \= catastrophic overfitting.

* Data leakage: Compute scaling parameters (mean, std) ONLY on the training split. Apply those same parameters to validation and test.

### **4E — Data Augmentation**

* Images: random crop, horizontal flip, color jitter, rotation, cutout, mixup

* Text: back-translation (translate to another language and back), synonym replacement, random insertion/deletion

* Tabular: SMOTE (synthetic minority oversampling for class imbalance)

| 5 | Feature Engineering *Converting raw data into numbers the model can use* |
| :---: | :---- |

Feature engineering is where domain knowledge meets mathematics. A good feature set often beats a better model with a poor feature set.

### **5A — Define Your Key Actors**

First, identify the main entities in your system. Then define features for each:

| Actor | Example Features | Notes |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| User | Age, gender, location, account age, recent activity, historical preferences | Profile features \+ behavioral features |
| Item / Content | Category, tags, recency, quality signals, embedding | Static metadata \+ learned representations |
| Query (Search) | Query text embedding, query length, entity types, intent | Critical for search/retrieval systems |
| Context | Time of day, device, location, session length, day of week | Captures situational variation |
| Cross Features (User × Item) | User's past interactions with this item category, user-item similarity | Often the most predictive features |

### **5B — Feature Representation Techniques**

| Technique | Input Type | Output | When to Use |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| One-Hot Encoding | Categorical (low cardinality) | Binary vector | Gender (M/F/Other), device type — few distinct values |
| Embedding Layer | Categorical (high cardinality) | Dense float vector | User ID, city, video ID — thousands of distinct values |
| Bucketization \+ One-Hot | Numerical continuous | Binary vector | Age (0–10, 10–20, ...) to capture non-linearity |
| Min-Max / Z-score Scaling | Numerical continuous | Scaled float | Any numerical feature — required for neural networks |
| TF-IDF | Text | Sparse float vector | Keyword importance for document retrieval |
| Pre-trained Embeddings | Text / Image / Audio | Dense float vector (256–768d) | When semantic meaning matters — BERT, CLIP, Word2Vec |
| Positional Embedding | Sequence data | Position-aware vector | Transformers, any order-sensitive sequence model |

### 

### **5C — Text Feature Engineering Pipeline**

![][image2]

### **5D — Image Feature Engineering Pipeline**

![][image3]

| Raw image → Preprocessing → Feature extraction 1\. Decode: convert raw bytes to pixel array 2\. Resize: standardize to model input (e.g. 224×224 for ResNet) 3\. Normalize: scale pixel values to \[0,1\] or z-score normalize 4\. Augment (training only): random crop, flip, color jitter 5\. Extract features via pre-trained model:    \- CLIP visual encoder: great for semantic image understanding    \- ResNet / EfficientNet: strong for classification    \- SimCLR: self-supervised, good for limited labeled data    → Output: dense vector (512d or 2048d depending on model) |
| :---- |

### **5E — Static vs Dynamic Features**

| Type | What It Is | Storage | Latency |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Static Features | Pre-computed and stored — user profile, item metadata | Feature Store (Redis / DynamoDB) | \< 5ms lookup |
| Dynamic Features | Computed at request time — real-time context, session state | Computed inline in serving | \< 10ms compute |
| Pre-computed Embeddings | Stored model outputs for items (videos, products) | Vector DB / Feature Store | \< 5ms lookup |

| 6 | Model Development and Offline Evaluation *Choose, train, and evaluate the right model* |
| :---: | :---- |

### **6A — Model Selection Principle: Always Start Simple**

The KISS principle: Keep It Simple and Iterate. Never jump straight to Transformers when logistic regression might work. Build from simple to complex:

![][image4]

| Iteration path:   1\. Heuristics / Rule-based baseline (no model)   2\. Simple model: Logistic Regression or Decision Tree   3\. Gradient Boosting (XGBoost / LightGBM) for tabular data   4\. Simple Neural Network (feedforward / MLP)   5\. Complex architectures: CNN, RNN, Transformer   6\. Ensemble of models (usually last resort) Move to the next level ONLY if the simpler model doesn't meet requirements. |
| :---- |

### **6B — Model Selection Reference**

| Model | Best For | Pros | Cons |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Logistic Regression | Binary classification, tabular features | Fast, interpretable, no overfitting on small data | Cannot learn non-linear patterns |
| GBDT / XGBoost | Tabular data (structured features) | Excellent on tabular data, handles missing values, fast | Not ideal for image/text/sequence data |
| Random Forest | Tabular data, need interpretability | Robust, handles outliers, feature importance built in | Slower than GBDT at inference |
| Feedforward NN (MLP) | Tabular \+ combined features | Learns non-linear patterns, flexible | Needs more data than GBDT for tabular |
| CNN | Image classification, spatial patterns | Captures spatial hierarchy, parameter efficient | Needs GPU, large datasets |
| RNN / LSTM / GRU | Sequential data, time series | Handles variable-length sequences | Slow training, vanishing gradient issues |
| Transformer | Text, images, sequences, multimodal | State-of-the-art across almost all domains | Very compute-intensive, needs large data |
| Two-Tower NN | Retrieval, recommendation, similarity | Efficient ANN search, scalable to billions of items | Requires careful negative sampling |

### 

### 

### 

### 

### **6C — Loss Functions Reference**

| Loss Function | Task Type | Formula / Notes |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Binary Cross-Entropy | Binary classification | −(y log p \+ (1−y) log(1−p)) — standard for 0/1 labels |
| Categorical Cross-Entropy | Multi-class classification | −Σ yᵢ log pᵢ — use with softmax output |
| Focal Loss | Imbalanced classification | Down-weights easy examples, focuses on hard ones — great for fraud, content moderation |
| MSE (Mean Squared Error) | Regression | Σ(y − ŷ)² / n — sensitive to outliers |
| MAE (Mean Absolute Error) | Regression | Σ|y − ŷ| / n — robust to outliers |
| Huber Loss | Regression (robust) | MSE for small errors, MAE for large — best of both |
| Contrastive Loss | Metric learning, embeddings | Pulls similar pairs together, pushes dissimilar apart |
| InfoNCE / NT-Xent | Self-supervised contrastive learning | Used in SimCLR, CLIP — excellent for learning embeddings |

### **6D — Handling Class Imbalance**

* Oversample minority class: duplicate rare examples in training batches

* Undersample majority class: randomly drop common examples

* Weighted loss function: multiply minority class loss by weight W (e.g. W=10 for 10:1 imbalance)

* Focal loss: automatically focuses training on hard, misclassified examples

* SMOTE: generate synthetic minority examples (works best for tabular data)

### **6E — Hyperparameter Tuning**

* Grid search: exhaustive search over predefined parameter grid — best for small parameter spaces

* Random search: randomly sample parameter combinations — better than grid search for large spaces

* Bayesian optimization: uses past results to guide next trial — most efficient, best for expensive models

* Key hyperparameters: learning rate (most important), batch size, number of layers, hidden dimensions, dropout rate, weight decay

### **6F — Offline Evaluation Protocol**

* Always evaluate on a held-out test set that the model has NEVER seen during training or tuning

* Use stratified splits for imbalanced datasets

* For time-series data: test set must be strictly after training data in time

* Report multiple metrics — never rely on a single number

* Model calibration: predicted probabilities should match actual frequencies (use Platt scaling or isotonic regression if needed)

| 7 | Prediction Service *Serving predictions fast, reliably, and at scale* |
| :---: | :---- |

### **7A — Batch vs Online vs Hybrid Prediction**

| Mode | How It Works | Latency | Throughput | Best For |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Batch Prediction | Pre-compute predictions offline, store results, serve from DB | High (pre-computed) | Very High | Recommendations for known users, offline reports |
| Online Prediction | Compute prediction at request time | Low (\<100ms) | Limited by compute | Real-time personalization, safety, search |
| Hybrid | Batch for stable features \+ online for real-time context | Low | High | Netflix: batch video embeddings \+ online user context |

| Senior engineer insight: Most production systems are hybrid. Pre-compute everything that changes slowly (video embeddings, user embeddings). Compute only what must be fresh at request time (current session context, trending items). This gives you low latency AND low cost. |
| :---- |

### **7B — Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) Service**

For retrieval systems (search, recommendation candidate generation), you need to find the top-K most similar items from millions or billions of embeddings in milliseconds. Exact search is too slow — use ANN:

| ANN Library | Approach | Speed | Accuracy | Best For |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| FAISS (Meta) | Index-based flat, IVF, HNSW | Very Fast (GPU) | Very High | Large-scale, GPU available |
| ScaNN (Google) | Anisotropic quantization | Very Fast | High | Production recommendation systems |
| HNSW (hnswlib) | Hierarchical navigable small world graphs | Fast | High | Managed vector DBs (Pinecone, Weaviate) |
| Annoy (Spotify) | Random projection forests | Fast | Medium | Read-heavy, no GPU needed |

### **7C — On-Device / Edge AI**

When network latency, privacy, or cost prevents cloud inference, models run on the device itself. This requires model compression:

* Quantization: reduce weight precision from float32 to float16 or int8 → 2–4× smaller, 2–4× faster, minimal accuracy loss

* Pruning: remove unimportant weights (near-zero) → sparse model → smaller and faster

* Knowledge distillation: train a small 'student' model to mimic a large 'teacher' model → same behavior, fraction of size

* Factorization: decompose large weight matrices into smaller factor matrices → reduces parameters

### **7D — Serving Infrastructure**

* Model registry: versioned storage of trained models (MLflow, SageMaker Model Registry)

* Model server: serves inference requests (TorchServe, TensorFlow Serving, Triton Inference Server)

* Feature store: centralized store for pre-computed features (Feast, Tecton, Redis \+ DynamoDB)

* Prediction cache: cache results for repeated queries (Redis, Memcached) — huge cost savings at scale

| 8 | Online Testing and Model Deployment *Safely rolling out the model to real users* |
| :---: | :---- |

### **8A — Deployment Strategies**

| Strategy | How It Works | Risk Level | Best For |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Shadow Deployment | New model runs in parallel, results NOT shown to users — compare predictions silently | Zero | Validating a new model before any user exposure |
| Canary Release | New model serves a tiny fraction (1–5%) of traffic — monitor metrics closely | Very Low | First real user exposure with easy rollback |
| Blue-Green Deployment | Two identical environments — switch traffic instantly from old (blue) to new (green) | Low | Zero-downtime deployment with instant rollback |
| A/B Testing | Split users into control (old model) and treatment (new model) groups, and compare outcomes | Medium | Measuring causal impact of a new model on business metrics |
| Multi-Armed Bandits | Dynamically allocate more traffic to better-performing variants in real time | Low-Medium | When you want to both test AND optimize simultaneously |

### **8B — A/B Testing Protocol**

6. Formulate a null hypothesis: 'The new model has no effect on CTR'

7. Decide the minimum detectable effect: how big must the improvement be to matter?

8. Calculate required sample size using power analysis (typically 80% power, α=0.05)

9. Randomly assign users to control and treatment — ensure groups are comparable

10. Run the test until statistical significance is reached (do NOT stop early)

11. Analyze results: primary metrics, counter metrics, segment breakdowns

12. If results are positive: full rollout. If negative: rollback and investigate.

| Common A/B testing mistakes: Peeking problem: checking results before the test is done and stopping early inflates false positive rate Novelty effect: users behave differently when they see something new — run for long enough to see stable behavior Network effects: if one user's experience affects another's (social network), standard A/B is biased — use cluster randomization |
| :---- |

| 9 | Scaling, Monitoring, and Updates *Keeping the system healthy and continuously improving* |
| :---: | :---- |

### **9A — Scaling the ML System**

| Scaling Challenge | Solution | Notes |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| High prediction QPS | Horizontal scaling \+ load balancer \+ prediction cache | Cache hit rate of 80%+ dramatically reduces GPU load |
| Large model, slow inference | Model quantization, batched inference, multi-GPU serving | Batch size tuning critical for GPU utilization |
| Large training dataset | Distributed training: data parallelism (split data) or model parallelism (split model) | Use NCCL for GPU communication |
| Feature computation at scale | Pre-compute and store in Feature Store, async feature updates | Avoid recomputing expensive features at request time |
| Vector search at scale | FAISS/ScaNN with IVF indexing \+ product quantization | Compress 800M embeddings to fit in RAM with quantization |

### **9B — Monitoring: What to Track**

ML systems fail in two ways: software failures and ML-specific failures. Monitor both:

**Software System Metrics**

* Prediction latency: p50, p95, p99 — alert if p99 exceeds SLA

* Error rate: 4xx and 5xx response rates

* Throughput: requests per second — alert on sudden drops or spikes

* Infrastructure: CPU/GPU utilization, memory, disk I/O

**ML-Specific Metrics**

* Prediction distribution: monitor the distribution of model outputs — sudden shifts signal problems

* Feature distributions: monitor input feature statistics — data drift here before model degradation

* Online metrics dashboard: CTR, conversion rate, engagement — continuous monitoring

* Model staleness: track how long since last retraining — set maximum allowed staleness

### **9C — Distribution Shift (The Silent Model Killer)**

| Shift Type | What It Is | Detection Method | Fix |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Covariate Shift | Input feature distribution changes (X changes, P(Y|X) stays same) | Monitor feature statistics (mean, variance, KS test) | Retrain with recent data, importance weighting |
| Label Shift | Output distribution changes (P(Y) changes) | Monitor prediction distributions and actual labels | Relabel recent data, adjust class weights |
| Concept Shift | Relationship between X and Y changes (e.g. 'good video' means different thing) | Monitor online metrics degradation | Retrain from scratch with recent labeled data |

### **9D — Continuous Learning / Model Update Strategy**

| Update Frequency | When to Use | Cost | Freshness |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Real-time (online learning) | When user behavior changes within minutes (news, trending topics) | Very High | Maximum — model updates as events arrive |
| Daily retraining | Most production systems — balance of freshness and cost | Medium | Good — catches daily behavioral shifts |
| Weekly retraining | When data changes slowly (stable domains) | Low | Acceptable for slow-changing domains |
| Trigger-based retraining | Retrain when monitoring detects drift above threshold | Low (efficient) | Adaptive — retrain only when needed |

### **9E — Active Learning**

When labeled data is scarce and expensive, use active learning to get maximum value from human annotation budget:

* Uncertainty sampling: ask humans to label examples the model is most uncertain about (predicted probability near 0.5)

* Diversity sampling: select examples that are most different from already-labeled examples

* Boundary cases: focus annotation on examples near the decision boundary — highest information gain

# **Common ML System Design Interview Questions**

These are the most frequently asked questions in ML engineering interviews at top tech companies. Use the 9-Step formula on each.

## **Recommendation Systems**

* Video/Movie recommendation (YouTube, Netflix) — Two-Tower \+ ANN retrieval \+ ranking

* Friends / follower recommendation (Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn) — Graph embeddings \+ PYMK

* Event recommendation (Eventbrite) — Location \+ time \+ user interest features

* Game recommendation — Behavioral sequence modeling

* Product replacement recommendation (Instacart) — Category \+ substitution embeddings

## **Search Systems (Retrieval \+ Ranking)**

* Text query search — BM25 \+ semantic search \+ Learning-to-Rank

* Image / video search — Visual embeddings \+ multimodal query

* Multimodal search — Joint text-image embedding space (CLIP-style)

## **Ranking Systems**

* Newsfeed ranking (Facebook, Twitter) — Engagement prediction \+ diversity \+ freshness

* Ads click prediction — Feature crossing \+ probability calibration \+ auction

* Ads serving system — Targeting \+ retrieval \+ bid prediction \+ ranking

## **NLP Systems**

* Autocompletion / typeahead — N-gram \+ neural language model

* Sentiment analysis — Text classification, BERT fine-tuning

* Named entity recognition — Sequence labeling with CRF or Transformer

* Chatbot / Question answering — Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)

* Language identification — Lightweight text classifier

## **Computer Vision Systems**

* Image classification — CNN / ViT

* Object detection — YOLO, Faster R-CNN

* Image blurring system (privacy) — Segmentation \+ face/plate detection

* OCR / text recognition — CNN \+ CTC decoder

## **Safety and Trust Systems**

* Harmful content detection — Multimodal, multi-task classification (violence, nudity, hate speech)

* Spam detection — Text \+ behavioral features \+ graph features

* Fraud detection — Tabular features \+ graph neural network

* Fake account detection — Behavioral signals \+ network graph analysis

# **Quick Reference Cheat Sheet**

## **When to Use Which Loss Function**

| Problem | Loss Function |
| ----- | ----- |
| Binary classification (balanced) | Binary Cross-Entropy |
| Binary classification (imbalanced) | Focal Loss or Weighted Binary Cross-Entropy |
| Multi-class (one label per example) | Categorical Cross-Entropy \+ Softmax |
| Multi-label (multiple labels per example) | Binary Cross-Entropy per label (sigmoid output) |
| Multi-task (multiple tasks simultaneously) | Weighted sum of per-task losses |
| Regression (outlier-sensitive) | MSE |
| Regression (outlier-robust) | MAE or Huber Loss |
| Embedding / metric learning | Contrastive Loss or InfoNCE |

## **When to Use Which Evaluation Metric**

| Situation | Metric to Use |
| ----- | ----- |
| Balanced binary classification | F1, ROC-AUC |
| Imbalanced binary classification (rare positives) | PR-AUC — more informative than ROC-AUC |
| Ranking quality (graded relevance) | nDCG |
| Ranking quality (binary relevance) | mAP, Precision@K |
| First correct result matters most | MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) |
| Translation / text generation quality | BLEU, ROUGE |
| Calibration (do probabilities match reality?) | Log-loss, Brier score, reliability diagram |

## **Feature Encoding Decision Tree**

| Feature Type | Cardinality | Encoding |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Categorical | Low (\< 10 values) | One-Hot Encoding |
| Categorical | Medium (10–1000 values) | Embedding Layer (learned) |
| Categorical | High (\> 1000 values) | Embedding Layer (learned) |
| Numerical (continuous) | N/A | Min-Max scaling or Z-score normalization |
| Numerical (non-linear relationship) | N/A | Bucketization \+ One-Hot |
| Text | N/A | Pre-trained BERT / DistilBERT embedding |
| Image | N/A | Pre-trained CNN / CLIP encoder |
| Video | N/A | Frame sampling \+ VideoMoCo / Video Transformer |

# 

# 

# **Reference and Further Reading**

| Primary Reference: This document is based on the Machine Learning Interviews repository by Alireza Dirafzoon, which covers ML system design, ML fundamentals, and behavioral interview preparation in comprehensive depth. |
| :---- |
